name: 5. COCO YOLO Convolutional Neural Network (PyTorch)
description: A YOLOv2-style object detection network built from scratch with PyTorch, trained on the MS COCO 2017 dataset. The model predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities on a 13×13 grid using a 26-layer backbone and a custom multi-component loss function. The model successfully learned to detect objects but overfit the training set — val loss bottomed at 4.21 on epoch 8 then rose to 5.97 by epoch 21 while train loss continued falling to 1.97.
architecture: 416×416×3 → Conv(32,3×3)+BN+SiLU → MaxPool(2×2) → Conv(64,3×3)+BN+SiLU → MaxPool(2×2) → Conv(128)+Conv(64)+Conv(128)+BN+SiLU → MaxPool(2×2) → Conv(256)+Conv(128)+Conv(256)+BN+SiLU → MaxPool(2×2) → Conv(512)×3+Conv(256)×2+BN+SiLU → MaxPool(2×2) → Conv(1024)×3+Conv(512)×2+BN+SiLU → Conv(1024,3×3)+BN+SiLU → Conv(1024,3×3)+BN+SiLU → Conv(170,1×1) → 13×13×170
techniques: YOLOv2-style grid-based object detection (S=13, B=2, C=80)
Custom YOLO loss with 5 components (objectness, no-object, xy, wh, class)
Per-cell loss normalisation (sum/num_obj) to avoid 300× dilution from full-grid mean
BCEWithLogitsLoss for objectness/xy/class, MSE on log scale for width/height
2 anchor boxes per cell matching COCO object aspect ratios
Batch Normalisation + SiLU activation on all backbone layers

results.summary: The model learned real object detections — objectness confidence reached ~86% on object cells by epoch 8 and all loss components improved. Val loss fell from 6.26 to 4.21 over 8 epochs then rose to 5.97 by epoch 21 while train continued falling to 1.97. The classification term drove the divergence, indicating the model memorised class-appearance patterns rather than generalising. Root cause: no data augmentation, no weight decay, and no early stopping with a ~30M parameter model.
